title: Making paper for printmaking
subtitle: Pulling and sizing sheets suited to linocut, monoprint, and block printing
excerpt: Handmade paper and printmaking are natural companions, but a sheet that works beautifully as writing paper may absorb ink too fast for a clean block print. Sizing with gelatine reduces absorbency, sharpens printed edges, and produces a surface that takes the ink from a linocut or woodblock without feathering.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft (1943, public domain via Internet Archive), on surface sizing with gelatine to prepare sheets for printing. Hunter documents the gelatine-sizing practice used in European printing papers from the fifteenth century onward.

glossaryTerms.definition: The application of a warm gelatine solution to a dry handmade sheet. The gelatine fills the spaces between surface fibres, reducing absorbency and allowing ink to sit on the surface rather than spreading into the sheet.
The evenness of fibre distribution across a sheet, visible when held up to light. Good formation produces a uniform translucency; poor formation shows thick and thin patches that print unevenly.
The spreading of ink along paper fibres beyond the intended edge of a printed mark, caused by excessive absorbency in the sheet surface.

content.text: A printmaking sheet needs two things an ordinary handmade sheet does not: consistent
and a surface-sized finish. Uneven fibre distribution prints as light and dark patches in solid areas. Unsized paper absorbs block-printing ink or relief ink too quickly, and the printed line
at the edges. Both problems are solved in the making: pull the sheet slowly for good formation, then apply
once the sheet is fully dry.

items.name: Cotton linter or linen rag pulp
items.notes: Cotton linter produces the smoothest surface for fine detail. Linen rag gives a slight texture that suits woodblock.

items.name: Papermaking vat
items.notes: A wide, low-sided tub. Keep the vat well-stirred for even fibre suspension.

items.name: Couching cloths and press boards
items.notes: For pressing the post before sizing.

items.name: Gelatine size
items.notes: Dissolve two leaf-gelatin sheets in 500 ml of hot water. Use warm, not boiling: boiling weakens the bond.

items.name: Flat brush, 50 mm wide
items.notes: For brushing the warm size onto the dry sheet.

content.text: Keep the pulp concentration lower than usual for writing paper: 1 to 1.5% fibre by volume gives a thinner suspension that distributes more evenly across the mould. Dip the mould slowly and lift with a deliberate shake to settle the fibres flat. Agitate the vat after every second pull to prevent fibre settling at the bottom. Hold the mould level while the water drains; any tilt at this stage creates a thick-thin gradient across the sheet.
Press the post overnight under weight, then peel and air-dry the sheets flat. The sheet must be completely dry before sizing; any residual moisture dilutes the gelatine and leaves soft spots.

Warm the gelatine solution to around 45°C. It should be fluid but not steaming. Lay the dry sheet on a clean board and brush the warm size across the surface with long overlapping strokes, working in one direction. Turn the sheet over and size the reverse. Hang to dry on a line or lay flat on a drying board. The sheet will be slightly stiffer and more translucent when dry: this is the gelatine fill closing the fibre interstices. Allow 24 hours before printing.

items.symptom: The sized sheet is stiff and buckled
items.cause: Too much gelatine applied, or the sheet dried unevenly.
items.fix: Thin the gelatine solution with a little warm water. Dry the sized sheet under a light weight on a smooth board.

items.symptom: The block-printed ink still feathers after sizing
items.cause: The size was not allowed to dry fully, or a second coat is needed.
items.fix: Allow an extra 24 hours drying time. Apply a second thin coat of size if the sheet still feels absorbent.
